_source: Roach, A., Schwartz, M.F., Martin, N., Grewal, R.S., & Brecher, A. (1996). The Philadelphia Naming Test: Scoring and rationale. Clinical Aphasiology, 24, 121-133.
_note: 175 picture-naming target words. Published as a public research instrument. Error type distributions per item are reported in Schwartz et al. (2006) Brain & Language and Mirman et al. (2010) Cognitive Neuropsychology.

_error_types.semantic_coordinate: A word from the same semantic category (cat -> dog)
_error_types.circumlocution: A descriptive phrase without the target word
_error_types.associative: A word functionally or contextually linked but not coordinate
_error_types.phonological: A word sharing sound structure with the target

semantic_error_rates._source: Schwartz, M.F. et al. (2006). Structural relationships underlying impairment in word production. Brain and Language, 99(3), 289-301.
semantic_error_rates._note: Rates from 94 participants with aphasia. Semantic errors total ~27% of all responses — the primary target for ReVoice's category expansion scoring.

revoice_relevance.note: The PNT tests isolated naming of common objects. ReVoice handles the same error types but applied to personal memory concepts (people, places, events). The semantic error taxonomy is identical: a person with anomia who cannot retrieve 'Lily' may say 'granddaughter' (superordinate), 'Sarah' (coordinate), or 'the one who calls on Sundays' (circumlocution) — exactly what _CATEGORY_EXPANSIONS handles.

category_mapping.animals: no direct mapping (not a ReVoice concept category)
category_mapping.food_kitchen: order (if personal, e.g. usual cafe order)
category_mapping.furniture_home: place (if a specific room or building)
